title: Meyniel's Conjecture
statement_text: For every connected graph $G$ of order $n$, $c(G) \leq O(\sqrt{n})$.
context_text: This fundamental conjecture about cop number appeared in a paper by Frankl [8] as a personal communication from Henri Meyniel in 1985. The paper proves that the 'weak' Meyniel's conjecture holds for expander graph families of bounded degree, as a byproduct of computing an upper bound on the cop number of Ramanujan graphs.
paper_contribution: Proves that the 'weak' Meyniel's conjecture holds for expander graph families of bounded degree.
paper_title: On the cop number of graphs of high girth

title: Conjecture 1.3 (Folklore)
statement_text: For any positive integers $g$ and $d$, there exists a $d$-regular graph $G$ of girth at least $g$, and order $\Theta\!\left((d-1)^{\left(\frac{1}{2}+o(1)\right)g}\right)$.
context_text: This folklore conjecture is related to the Moore bound and generalises a conjecture of Bollobás and Szemerédi for cubic graphs. The paper invokes it, together with Meyniel's conjecture, to argue that no lower bound of the form $\Omega((d-1)^{cg})$ with $c > \frac{1}{4}$ can hold for all graphs of girth $g$ and minimum degree $d$.
attributed_to: Folklore (Bollobás and Szemerédi for the cubic case)
paper_contribution: Uses this conjecture conditionally to support the claim that the exponent $\frac{1}{4}$ in their lower bound is best possible; does not prove the conjecture itself.
notes: PDF source — the exponent '( 1 2 +o(1))g' appears garbled; interpreted as $\left(\tfrac{1}{2}+o(1)\right)g$.
paper_title: On the cop number of graphs of high girth

title: Optimality of the exponent 1/4
statement_text: Is the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ of $g$ in the lower bound $\Omega\!\left((\delta-1)^{g/4}\right)$ for the cop number of graphs of girth $g$ and minimum degree $\delta$ best possible, i.e., can the exponent be improved to $\left(\frac{1}{4}+\varepsilon\right)g$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$?
context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.1, the authors note: 'One may naturally ask if the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ of $g$ is best possible. While we do not have an answer to this question …'. They exhibit conditional evidence (via Meyniel's conjecture and Conjecture 1.3) that $\frac{1}{4}$ is optimal, and also prove via Ramanujan graphs that the true exponent cannot exceed $\frac{3}{8}$.
notes: Stated as prose without a labelled environment; the abstract frames it as 'several reasons for conjecturing that the exponent $\frac{1}{4}g$ cannot be improved to $(\frac{1}{4}+\varepsilon)g$'.
paper_title: On the cop number of graphs of high girth
